response: Enacted in May 2018, the EU’s GDPR fundamentally reshaped data privacy by requiring strict user consent, 72-hour breach reporting, and expansive data rights for any organization processing EU residents' information. Noncompliance carries penalties of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, resulting in landmark fines against Meta (€1.2 billion), Amazon (€746 million), and Google (over €200 million). While critics argue the regulation imposes disproportionate compliance costs on small businesses and suffers from uneven enforcement, it has successfully established data privacy as a fundamental right. Consequently, the GDPR has become the global benchmark, directly inspiring similar data protection laws in California, Brazil, and India.
